header_summary:
The [btc-verified project](https://github.com/ProofOfKeags/btc-verified) has made strides in applying Lean4 for the formal verification of Bitcoin, enhancing the clarity of the protocol’s functionality and addressing factual discrepancies in its design. This method provides precise definitions and proofs, particularly demonstrating the unique correspondence between a Merkle Root and its tree, essential for maintaining the integrity of block transactions. Despite its detailed approach, the project admits to limitations in mirroring the exact behavior of Bitcoin Core in real-world scenarios, thus not serving as a definitive guide but offering valuable insights into the protocol's descriptive accuracy.

New release candidates for Bitcoin Core have been announced: version v29.4rc1 and v30.3rc1 are available for community testing, with binaries accessible at [Bitcoin Core’s official site for v29.4rc1](https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-29.4/test.rc1/) and [for v30.3rc1](https://bitcoincore.org/bin/bitcoin-core-30.3/test.rc1/). These versions aim to detect and resolve critical issues prior to their final releases, with the community encouraged to report any problems through GitHub at [Report Issues for v29.4rc1](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues) and [for v30.3rc1](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues), enhancing the software's stability and security through collaborative revision.

The discussions within the community also touched on broader issues, such as the evolving threat landscape for Bitcoin, emphasizing concerns over miner consolidation and its implications for network control and stability. While speculative forces and potential quantum computing advancements shape Bitcoin's future, the dialogue reflects ongoing uncertainty around the economic and security ramifications of a possibly centralized Bitcoin model, highlighting the critical role of community feedback and peer review in navigating these challenges.

recent_posts.title: btc-verified: formalizing the Bitcoin protocol
recent_posts.summary: - The Bitcoin protocol development in Lean4 focuses on verifying intricate system details.  - Lean4's use enhances certainty in Bitcoin's operations through specific logical assertions.  - The project reveals a critical correspondence between Merkle Roots and their trees in Bitcoin's architecture.

recent_posts.title: Bitcoin Core v29.4rc1 is now available
recent_posts.summary:
- Bitcoin Core's version v29.4rc1 is now available for download.
- The source code is accessible via a signed tag on GitHub for transparency.
- Issues with this release can be reported on the project's GitHub issue tracker.

recent_posts.title: Bitcoin Core v30.3rc1 is now available
recent_posts.summary:
- Bitcoin Core version v30.3rc1 is now available for download and testing.
- Release notes and source code are accessible on GitHub, detailing changes and improvements.
- Users can report issues with this release candidate via the project’s GitHub page.

recent_posts.title: Input-triggered transaction expiry
recent_posts.summary:
- An error in a proposed idea was identified, leading to its retraction.
- The error was spotted after another party challenged the original proposal.
- This emphasizes the importance of peer review in problem-solving and error correction.

recent_posts.title: Addressing the Diminishing Block Subsidy
recent_posts.summary:
- The discussion addresses the changing security and economic stability threats to Bitcoin.
- Concerns include miner consolidation's impact on network control and potential centralization.
- Future scenarios might lead to severe measures like a miner whitelist to maintain integrity.

recent_posts.title: Bitcoin Core v31.1rc1 is now available
recent_posts.summary:
- Bitcoin Core version v31.1rc1 is now available for download as a test release.
- Source code and binaries for the release candidate are accessible on GitHub and Bitcoin Core's website.
- Community feedback through testing and issue reporting is crucial for the upcoming official release.

active_posts.title: Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
active_posts.summary:
- The proposal modifies BIP360 to mandate merkle paths in P2MR, enhancing security.
- It aims to eliminate depth-zero script trees, promoting more secure spending conditions.
- Further consultations are planned to assess the impact on privacy and fungibility.

active_posts.title: Prohibit Merkle Internal Node Preimages That Encode Minimal 64-Byte Transactions
active_posts.summary:
- The proposal introduces a new Bitcoin rule targeting specific 64-byte transactions.
- It aims to prevent SPV malleability by invalidating blocks with certain transaction structures.
- Implementation guidance includes a C++ function and miner procedures for block validation.

active_posts.title: Giving teeth to expected EC disabling: P2XX(-T)(-ML)
active_posts.summary: - The Bitcoin community is exploring new security protocols for output types like P2TRv2.  - Proposals like Tripwire and Miner Lockdown aim to disable EC opcodes against quantum threats.  - These integrated mechanisms bolster network security and uphold its decentralized ethos.

active_posts.title: [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage
active_posts.summary:
- SegData proposes efficient data storage in Bitcoin blocks via soft-fork, using a distinct Merkle root.
- It allows optional data pruning post-validation and distributes storage costs among opting operators.
- The technology supports non-transactional data like indexers and timestamping services, but not script-dependent types.

active_posts.title: State of the transaction privacy work in Bitcoin
active_posts.summary:
- Bitcoin privacy focuses on sidechains, L2s, and wallet integrations, not Layer 1.
- Technologies like Confidential Transactions and Silent Payments enhance wallet privacy.
- The Lightning Network reduces the need for direct Layer 1 privacy enhancements.

active_posts.title: SHRINCS: 324-byte stateful post-quantum signatures with static backups
active_posts.summary:
- SHRINCS combines stateful and stateless signature techniques for robustness and efficiency.
- It utilizes dual-mode functionality with seamless transitions based on state integrity.
- The design includes unbalanced XMSS trees and WOTS+C for optimized secure operations.

today_in_history_posts.title: BIP Process and Votes
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- The email discusses challenges with hard forks as a distraction in Bitcoin maintenance.
- Tier Nolan highlights the lack of a structured process for hard forks unlike soft forks.
- A proposal includes scheduled hard forks and adding steps to BIP to include broader input.

today_in_history_posts.title: Payment Re-routing
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- The Lightning Network lacks a central processor for enforcing node reputation.
- Trust issues arise when nodes must be highly available without central oversight.
- Possible solutions include rerouting payments or implementing a rating system.

today_in_history_posts.title: Fingerprinting nodes via addr requests
today_in_history_posts.summary:
- The blog post analyzes a fingerprint attack on nodes via `ADDR` responses, highlighting significant privacy and network threats.
- Research conducted on Bitcoin Core nodes version 0.21 or later showed varying response behaviors on IPv4 and Tor networks.
- Proposed mitigation strategies include altering `ADDR` messages to protect against cross-network node correlation.
